title: Hoya rundumensis
taxonomy: Hoya rundumensis is classified as a species of flowering plant in the family Apocynaceae. This family is a group of about 2,000 species of flowering plants, including the dogbane and oleander. The genus Hoya is a group of about 500 species, commonly known as the wax plants or hoya. Hoya rundumensis is native to the tropical regions of the Pacific Islands, specifically the island of Rundum, Vanuatu. As a member of the genus Hoya, it is a type of evergreen climbing plant that is known for its fragrant and showy flowers.

Additionally, Hoya rundumensis is thought to have antibacterial and antifungal properties, making it a potential natural remedy for various infections. Further research is needed to fully understand the extent of its health benefits.

side_effects: Hoya rundumensis is known to be non-toxic to humans and animals, but it's essential to note that consuming any part of the plant can cause gastrointestinal issues. intro: Hoya rundumensis is a species of flowering plant in the family Apocynaceae, commonly known as the waxflower or hoya. Hoya rundumensis has been traditionally used in medicine to treat various health issues, including fever, rheumatism, and respiratory problems, due to its potential anti-inflammatory, antibacterial, and antifungal properties, which provide relief and promote healing. The bioactive constituents of Hoya rundumensis include alkaloids, flavonoids, and glycosides, such as vincoside, which is responsible for its medicinal properties.
